title: Collections: ArrayList, LinkedList, HashMap & HashSet
description: Master Java's most exam-critical collection types — the data structures that appear in virtually every CSE110 practical and written question involving dynamic data.

sections.content: 📚 Why Collections Matter in CSE110 Exams

Java's Collections Framework is tested in CSE110 in two ways: **conceptual questions** (which collection to use and why) and **code tracing questions** (predict output from a sequence of add/remove/get operations). Understanding the difference between List, Map, and Set, and knowing the method names for each, directly decides marks on written and practical exams.

sections.label: The import Statement
sections.content: All standard collections require `import java.util.*;` at the top of your file. Without it, you get a compile-time error. In exams, always write this import even if not prompted.
🗃️ The Collections Hierarchy — Big Picture

Java collections implement two main interfaces: **Collection** (for groups of elements: List, Set, Queue) and **Map** (for key-value pairs). The most exam-relevant types are `ArrayList` (indexed list), `LinkedList` (doubly-linked, also a Deque), `HashMap` (key→value mapping), and `HashSet` (unique elements).
📋 ArrayList — Dynamic Array

`ArrayList<E>` is backed by a resizable array. It gives O(1) **random access** by index (like an array), but O(N) insertion/deletion in the middle (elements must shift). It is the most commonly used collection in Java and the one most frequently tested in CSE110.

sections.title: ArrayList — Complete Method Reference

sections.label: remove(int) vs remove(Object) — Most Common Exam Mistake
sections.content: For `ArrayList<Integer>`, calling `list.remove(2)` removes the element **at index 2**, NOT the element with value 2. To remove the value 2, you must write `list.remove(Integer.valueOf(2))`. This is a classic CSE110 trap in predict-output questions — a wrong answer here loses multiple marks.
🔗 LinkedList — Doubly-Linked List

`LinkedList<E>` implements both `List` and `Deque`. It excels at O(1) insertions and deletions at the **head or tail**, making it ideal for queue/stack operations. However, `get(i)` is O(N) because it must traverse from the front. Use `LinkedList` when you frequently add/remove from both ends.

sections.title: LinkedList as Queue and Stack

sections.content: 🗺️ HashMap — Key-Value Mapping

`HashMap<K, V>` stores key-value pairs with O(1) average time for `put`, `get`, and `containsKey`. Keys must be unique; values can repeat. It does **not** guarantee insertion order. For ordered iteration, use `TreeMap` (sorted by key) or `LinkedHashMap` (insertion order).

sections.title: HashMap — Complete Method Reference

sections.label: HashMap Iteration Order is Unpredictable
sections.content: When you iterate over a `HashMap` with `entrySet()` or `keySet()`, the order is **NOT guaranteed** to be insertion order or alphabetical. In predict-output questions, if the answer depends on iteration order of a HashMap, the exact output is implementation-dependent. If a question requires consistent order, a `TreeMap` (sorted) or `LinkedHashMap` (insertion-ordered) should be used.
🔵 HashSet — Unique Elements

`HashSet<E>` stores unique elements with O(1) `add`, `remove`, and `contains`. It is backed by a `HashMap`. Order is not guaranteed. The most common CSE110 use: checking membership, deduplication of a list, and set-theoretic operations (union, intersection).

sections.title: HashSet — Operations and Set Logic

sections.content: 🔁 Iterating Collections
There are four standard ways to iterate collections in Java. Exams test all of them and may ask you to predict output from any variation. Know the syntax cold.

sections.title: Four Ways to Iterate a List

sections.label: ConcurrentModificationException
sections.content: Never add or remove elements from an `ArrayList` while iterating it with a for-each loop. This throws a **runtime** `ConcurrentModificationException`. To safely remove during iteration, use an `Iterator` and call `it.remove()`, or collect items to remove in a separate list and remove after the loop.
🛠️ Collections Utility Class
The `Collections` class (note: not `Collection`) provides static utility methods that operate on collection objects. These appear frequently in exam code snippets.
🏋️ Interactive Exercises

sections.title: Exercise 1: ArrayList Operations Trace
sections.explanation: Step-by-step: After 3 adds → [10, 20, 30]. After `add(1, 15)` → [10, 15, 20, 30] (15 inserted at index 1, others shift right). After `remove(0)` → [15, 20, 30] (removes element at index 0 which is 10). After `set(1, 25)` → [15, 25, 30] (replaces index 1). `size()` = 3. `get(0)` = 15.

sections.title: Exercise 2: HashMap Frequency Count Trace
sections.explanation: The loop counts word frequency using `getOrDefault`. After processing all words: cat=3 (appears 3 times), dog=2, bird=1. `get("cat")` = 3. `get("dog")` = 2. `containsKey("fish")` = false (not in the array). `size()` = 3 (three distinct keys: cat, dog, bird).

sections.title: Exercise 3: HashSet Deduplication and Membership
sections.explanation: The array has 10 elements but duplicates: 1 appears twice, 5 appears twice, 3 appears twice. Unique elements: {1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6, 9} = 7 elements. `size()` = 7. `contains(7)` = false (7 is not in the array). After `remove(4)`: {1, 2, 3, 5, 6, 9}, `contains(4)` = false. `size()` = 6.

sections.title: Exercise 4: ConcurrentModification During Iteration
sections.error: Line 5 (`list.remove(x)` inside enhanced for-loop): Runtime ConcurrentModificationException. Modifying an ArrayList while iterating it with a for-each loop is forbidden — the iterator detects structural modification and throws this exception immediately.
sections.explanation: Java's ArrayList uses a `modCount` counter. The iterator checks this counter on every `next()` call. When `list.remove()` is called outside the iterator, `modCount` changes, and the iterator throws `ConcurrentModificationException` to prevent undefined behavior. Fix: use `Iterator.remove()` which updates `modCount` internally and is safe. Output after fix: [1, 3, 5].

sections.title: Exercise 5: Word Frequency — Find Most Common Word
sections.approach: Use a HashMap<String, Integer> to count frequency of each word. Then iterate the map's entrySet to find the key with the maximum value. Two-pass approach: first pass builds the frequency map, second pass finds the maximum.
sections.explanation: Time: O(N) for both passes. Space: O(K) where K = number of distinct words. The `getOrDefault(w, 0) + 1` pattern is the standard Java idiom for frequency counting — commit this to memory as it appears in dozens of exam variations. Always iterate `entrySet()` when you need both key and value simultaneously, as it's more efficient than calling `get()` for each key separately.

sections.content: 📝 Quick Reference Summary

items: **ArrayList** — dynamic array; O(1) get by index; `add(val)`, `add(idx,val)`, `remove(idx)`, `remove(Object)`, `set(idx,val)`, `get(idx)`, `size()`, `contains(val)`, `clear()`, `isEmpty()`
**LinkedList** — doubly-linked; O(1) at ends; `addFirst`, `addLast`, `pollFirst`, `pollLast`, `peekFirst`, `peekLast`; also implements Deque/Stack via `push`/`pop`
**Import required**: `import java.util.*;` — always include this, never forget
**remove() trap**: `list.remove(2)` removes by index; `list.remove(Integer.valueOf(2))` removes by value — distinguish carefully
**Iteration safety**: never call `list.remove()` inside a for-each loop — use `Iterator.remove()` or `removeIf()` instead
**HashMap order**: not guaranteed; use `TreeMap` for sorted key order or `LinkedHashMap` for insertion order
**Frequency pattern**: `map.put(key, map.getOrDefault(key, 0) + 1)` — the single most tested HashMap idiom in CSE110
